messages.content:
Please modify this section: Arrangement of the dissertation:

Preliminaries: this precedes the text and should be as follows :
1.	. The first page of the Dissertation is the title page. Title page: should contain;
		<Title of dissertation>
		<Full name of candidate>
The statement, “A dissertation submitted to the University of Colombo in partial fulfillment of the requirements for the Degree of Master of Science in Biochemistry and Molecular Biology” 
	Supervisors; <name of supervisor>
		         <name of additional supervisors – if any>
and centered, at the bottom of the page;
Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology
Faculty of Medicine, University of Colombo
< Month and year of submission >


2.	The page following the title page shall carry the declaration by the student as given in below.
 Statement of originality:
“I declare that the work presented in this dissertation is my own, and that no part of the dissertation has been submitted earlier or concurrently for any other degree”.
The statement should be signed by the author and countersigned by the supervisor/s
3.	Abstract :  
A brief summary of your work (maximum 500 words).  The abstract should consist of a description of the objectives, the methods used, the results obtained and the conclusions drawn. 
4.	Acknowledgements: This should be brief and only key persons to be acknowledged identified by name. Any funding bodies should also be acknowledged here.
5.	Table of contents: should include all relevant divisions of the dissertation, including the preliminary pages and annexures.
6.	List of tables (if any):  titles of the tables, in the order they occur in the text, with the page numbers.
7.	List of figures (if any): titles of the figures, in the order they occur in the text, with the page numbers.

Arrangement of the dissertation:

Preliminaries: This section precedes the main text and should be organized as follows:

1. Title page: This should be the first page of the dissertation and should contain the following information:
- Title of the dissertation
- Full name of the candidate
- The statement, "A dissertation submitted to the University of Colombo in partial fulfillment of the requirements for the Degree of Master of Science in Biochemistry and Molecular Biology"
- Supervisors: Name of the supervisor
- Additional supervisors (if any)
- Centered at the bottom of the page:
  Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology
  Faculty of Medicine, University of Colombo
  Month and year of submission

2. Declaration: The page following the title page should feature a declaration by the student, as provided below:
"Statement of originality:
I declare that the work presented in this dissertation is my own, and that no part of the dissertation has been submitted earlier or concurrently for any other degree."
The statement should be signed by the author and countersigned by the supervisor(s).

3. Abstract: This section should consist of a brief summary of the work (maximum 500 words). It should include a description of the objectives, methods used, results obtained, and conclusions drawn.

4. Acknowledgements: This section should be brief and only mention key individuals by name who have contributed significantly to the dissertation. Any funding bodies should also be acknowledged here.

5. Table of contents: This should provide a list of all relevant divisions of the dissertation, including the preliminary pages and annexures.

6. List of tables (if any): This section should list the titles of the tables, in the order they appear in the text, along with the corresponding page numbers.

7. List of figures (if any): This section should list the titles of the figures, in the order they appear in the text, along with the corresponding page numbers.

Modify this part: This section should contain a comprehensive review of the literature relevant to your work. Most of the references would be in this section and should be up to date. References to the most recently published work are essential for a good thesis.
The chapter 1 should end with the rationale for your research/justification for the study and the list of objectives. State briefly the current position of the area of your study, noting the research gap / and the reasons for carrying out the present work. This section should end with the list of objectives (general and specific).

Chapter 1: Literature Review and Rationale

This chapter should provide a comprehensive review of the literature that is relevant to your work. It is important to include up-to-date references and emphasize the most recently published work. These references will serve as the foundation for a sound thesis.

The chapter should culminate with a clear rationale for your research and a justification for carrying out the study. Briefly state the current state of the field of study, highlighting any research gaps or areas that need further investigation. Explain the reasons for conducting the present work, addressing the significance and potential impact of the study.

Additionally, this section should conclude with a list of objectives, both general and specific, that outline what you aim to achieve through your research. These objectives should be clearly defined and aligned with the research gap identified.
Write 80 words about a meterails and methods of a thesis

The materials and methods section of a thesis provides a detailed description of the materials, techniques, and procedures used in the research. It should include information about the study design, sample size, data collection methods, instrumentation, and statistical analyses. The section should be concise yet comprehensive, allowing other researchers to replicate the study. Clear and specific explanations of the materials and methods employed are essential to ensure the validity and reliability of the research findings.
Write 80 words about results section of a thesis

The results section of a thesis presents the findings of the research in a clear and structured manner. It should provide a comprehensive and objective analysis of the data collected. The results should be presented in a logical order, using appropriate tables, charts, and figures to aid in understanding. The section should focus on key findings, highlighting any significant patterns, trends, or correlations observed. It is important to support the results with relevant statistical analyses and interpret the findings in relation to the research objectives and literature.
Write 80 words about the discussion section of a thesis

The discussion section of a thesis is where the researcher interprets and explains the significance of the results obtained. It provides an opportunity to delve into the implications of the findings, relating them to existing literature and theoretical frameworks. In this section, the researcher can analyze any discrepancies or unexpected results, offering possible explanations and alternative interpretations. It is crucial to address any limitations or weaknesses in the study and propose future directions for research. The discussion section allows for critical thinking and demonstrates the researcher's ability to contextualize their findings within the broader scientific landscape.

Modify this : References must be complete and accurate. Examiners will mark candidates down for inconsistencies and omissions. References should be listed in the ‘Harvard style’ (author –date’ system). All references should be listed in the section of references at the end of the Dissertation in alphabetical order of surname of the first author.

References should be thorough and precise to ensure accuracy. Inconsistencies and omissions may result in lower marks from examiners. It is important to use the 'Harvard style' (author-date system) when listing references. In the reference section at the end of the dissertation, all references should be arranged alphabetically by the surname of the first author.
Write 40 words about appendices of a thesis:

The appendices of a thesis contain supplementary information that complements the main text but is not crucial for understanding the research. This can include raw data, detailed methodology, questionnaires, or additional figures and tables.

Modify this: These should be numbered sequentially within each chapter (eg Table 2.1, Table 2.2 etc., if the Tables appear in  chapter 2) in Arabic numerals and arranged in the appropriate place in the text. Tables should have a brief title and each column and row a short abbreviated heading. All non-standard abbreviations used in each Table/Figure should be explained in a footnote. Each Figure should have a legend. Any Figures or Tables adopted from other sources should be indicated. 
Every table/ figure included must be referred to within the text (eg. as shown in Table 3.1). A Table and a Figure should be confined to one page. If a Table extends to more than one page, Title of the Table and column headings should be given on each page.

Tables and figures should be numbered sequentially within each chapter, using Arabic numerals (e.g., Table 2.1, Table 2.2) if they appear in Chapter 2. They should be placed in the appropriate location within the text. Each table should have a concise title, with columns and rows having short abbreviated headings. Non-standard abbreviations used in each table/figure should be explained in a footnote. Each figure should have a legend. If adopting figures or tables from other sources, proper acknowledgment should be provided. 
Every table/figure included must be referenced in the text (e.g., "as shown in Table 3.1"). Tables and figures should be confined to one page, and if a table extends to multiple pages, the title and column headings should be repeated on each page.

modify this: Do not use abbreviations in the title and in the abstract. The full term for which an abbreviation stands should precede its first use in the text, unless it is a standard unit of measurement. Abbreviations used should be generally accepted ones. All abbreviations used should be given in a list of abbreviations as indicated under item 8.

The use of abbreviations in the title and abstract should be avoided. When using abbreviations in the text, the full term they represent should be included before its first use, unless it is a standard unit of measurement. It is important to use generally accepted abbreviations. All abbreviations used should be listed in a separate section titled "List of Abbreviations," as indicated in item 8.

Modify this: Should be reported in metric units (e.g., m, kg, and L for height, weight, and volume, respectively), and in the Celsius scale (°C). For reporting data, use of SI units (le Systeme Internationale d’Unites) is preferred (e.g., mmol/L, g/L) but not mandatory. Conventional units such as mg/dL and mg/mL are acceptable, using L, not l, for liter. Use units for the same analyte/compound consistently throughout the dissertation.

All measurements should be reported using metric units, such as meters (m), kilograms (kg), and liters (L) for height, weight, and volume, respectively. Temperature should be reported in Celsius (°C). It is preferred, though not mandatory, to use SI units (Systeme Internationale d'Unites) for data reporting, such as mmol/L and g/L. However, conventional units like mg/dL and mg/mL are also acceptable, with the use of "L" (not "l") for liter. Consistency in using units for the same analyte/compound throughout the dissertation is important.
